Definition: In this task, summarize the TIS CSC clock services and interfaces.
Emphasis & Caution: Include initialization, synchronization, and retrieval functions.
Things to Avoid: Adding non-time-related functions.

High_Requirements: The image mode shall shift successive frames according to the calculated spacecraft drift and co-add the frame into an accumulated image.
Low_Requirements: Telemetry Output to the Spacecraft 1553 BusThe SCU, which is a MIL-STD-1553 Bus Controller (BC), periodically reads the STPDU contained in the fifteen telemetry subaddresses, and then writes to the telemetry done subaddress.  When the telemetry done subaddress is written, the scuiTlmDoneISR() is called which gives a semaphore to awaken the scuiTask().  The software provides fault tolerance by automatically awaking after approximately two polling periods.  This timeout is automatically adjusted any time the SCU Poll Rate value is changed by command.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how telemetry is completed and acknowledged on the 1553 bus.
Emphasis & Caution: Mention ISR triggering and timeout-based fault tolerance.
Things to Avoid: Ignoring poll rate effects.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall read from the DCI Memory in B-bit words only, using the load multiple/store multiple instructions.
Low_Requirements: Standard I/O InterfaceThe read() primitive does not block.  A read is performed on the active portion of the H/W ping pong buffer using the RAD 6000 move-multiple instruction, referred to here after as a block move.  For optimum performance, the block move is implemented in assembler.  The number of bytes read is returned to the caller.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain the behavior of the Standard I/O read() primitive.
Emphasis & Caution: Note non-blocking behavior and block move usage.
Things to Avoid: Claiming blocking reads.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall provide the capability to echo to the spacecraft each received command from the spacecraft or the ICU.
Low_Requirements: InitializationThe SCUI CSC is initialized when the scuiTask() is spawned by the CCM CSC.  Initialization parameters which are passed into the scuiTask() include:* Poll Rate  rate at which the S/C will poll the DPU for telemetry.  The rate is specified in Hz, and has a default of X Hz based on the INSTRUMENT X-DPU poll rate in the Company X 1553 ICD.* Buffer Rate  value used to rate buffer data to the S/C.  The rate is specified in bits per telemetry poll, and has a default of 7664 which is equal to one STPDU per poll (*** bytes per STPDU times V bits/byte).  This default value effectively disables rate buffering.* Size of Low Priority Queue  size in bytes of the low priority telemetry queue.  If the amount requested cannot be allocated, the buffer will be allocated at  the amount of memory contained in the largest free memory block.Size of High Priority Queue  size in bytes of the high priority telemetry queue. If the amount requested cannot be allocated, the buffer will be allocated at  the amount of memory contained in the largest free memory block.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize SCUI CSC initialization parameters and defaults.
Emphasis & Caution: Include poll rate, buffer rate, and queue sizing rules.
Things to Avoid: Assuming allocation always succeeds.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall provide an application program with the ability to configure the SCM_DCI_WIN parameters.
Low_Requirements: Command HandlingWhen a command arrives from the SCU (via the 1553 interface) or the ICU (via the SSI interface), the respective ISR will enqueue the command packet into a Command Queue, and then give the semaphore to awaken the ccmCmdTask().  Since it is possible for the DPU to send a command to itself, commands may arrive at interrupt context or task context.  Therefore the CCM maintains two queues  one for interrupt context which is not semaphore protected, and one for task context which is semaphore protected.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how incoming commands are queued and dispatched.
Emphasis & Caution: Distinguish interrupt and task context queues.
Things to Avoid: Assuming a single command queue.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall monitor the status of DPU-SCUI, DPU-CCM, DPU-DCX, DPU-TMALI, and DPU-DPA by examining their TASK_INFO and report this information in DPU_HK.
Low_Requirements: Bootstrap MonitorThe Bootstrap Monitor checks entered commands for syntax and number of arguments, and displays an error message to the RS-232 interface if an invalid command or argument is entered.  A complete listing of these messages is given in document 7384-BSPS-01.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how the Bootstrap Monitor validates commands and reports errors.
Emphasis & Caution: Mention syntax and argument checks only.
Things to Avoid: Listing full error messages.

High_Requirements: The Bootstrap Monitor of DPU-BOOT CSC shall be activated if the Clear-To-Send (CTS) signal is active on the RS-232 interface.   If no activity is detected on the RS-232 interface within 30 seconds, the Bootstrap shall load a configuration of DPU-RTOS according to BC_INDEX in the SYSTEM_BLOCK.
Low_Requirements: Built-In TestThere are two stages of Built-In Tests (BIT) for the DPU.  The Stage 1 BIT is included in the Bootstrap program and consists of Central Processing Unit (CPU) BIT, and Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM) BIT.  The Stage 2 BIT is executed after the operating system is booted and is not part of the Bootstrap program.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize the two-stage Built-In Test structure and timing.
Emphasis & Caution: Separate bootstrap and post-boot tests.
Things to Avoid: Mixing Stage 1 and Stage 2 roles.

High_Requirements: The DPU-TIS shall provide the capability for application programs to access the current UT_DELTA.
Low_Requirements: Time RetrievalThe DPU maintains a local copy of the spacecraft clock and current UT Delta in order that the DPU FSW can timestamp data products and packets.  The TIS CSC provides a function, tisTimeGet(), which retrieves a copy of both the current DPU clock value and the most recent UT Delta value.  Time is communicated among DPU FSW applications via the TIS_TIME data structure.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how current time and UT Delta are retrieved from the TIS CSC.
Emphasis & Caution: Mention the TIS_TIME data structure.
Things to Avoid: Describing time synchronization logic.

High_Requirements: The DPU-RTOS shall provide the capability to invoke user-installed Interrupt Service Routines (ISRs) in response to internal and external (VME) interrupts.
Low_Requirements: Hardware InterfacesThe DPU-BOOTSTRAP must interface directly with the EEPROM on the Company X Communication Module.  The DPU-BOOTSTRAP accesses EEPROM as ?-bit words only.  The specific timing requirements of the EEPROM are satisfied in one of three ways:* the timing requirements are met by inserting specific wait loops (typical),* the timing requirements are met by use of the EEPROM Driver (DOWNLOAD command), andthe timing requirements are met implicitly by the amount of time which will be required to execute the intervening instructions regardless of the CPU speed setting (DRAM test logic).
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how the DPU-BOOTSTRAP interfaces with the EEPROM and satisfies its timing requirements.
Emphasis & Caution: Access EEPROM only as fixed-width words and include all three timing methods.
Things to Avoid: Do not omit any timing mechanism.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall provide the capability for an application program to reset the DCI by writing to the DCI Reset Register.
Low_Requirements: DRAM BITPart of the memory is tested in Boot Sequence 1, and part of the memory is tested during Boot Sequence 2.  During Boot Sequence 1, ? KB of DRAM is tested before the Bootstrap is copied to this area.  During Boot Sequence 2, the  remaining memory is tested in ? KB blocks. The DRAM BIT first writes the address of each word in a block to that word in the block.   Then the test flushes the cache and reads back the entire block.  If a single-bit error occurs on the read-back, the location is read again.  If another single-bit error occurs, the test assumes a hard error and marks the whole block bad.  If a multiple-bit error occurs, the whole block is marked bad.  If the value read back does not match the address, the whole block is marked bad.  This same process is repeated with the ones complement of the address. The Bootstrap stores the results of the DRAM BIT in EEPROM as a series of x consecutive y-bit words.  Each bit in a result word represents the result of testing a ? KB block of DRAM.  These bits are mapped to DRAM by proceeding from least-significant bit (LSb) to most-significant bit (MSb) as the addresses of the memory blocks increase.  A set bit indicates that a block of DRAM has failed.  A clear bit indicates that a block of DRAM has passed the test.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize the DRAM BIT process across both boot sequences and how results are stored.
Emphasis & Caution: Clearly distinguish single-bit vs multiple-bit error handling and bit-to-block mapping.
Things to Avoid: Do not mix up boot sequences or error conditions.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall provide the capability for an application program to set a timeout in milliseconds that specifies a period during which no new events have been received by the hardware, after which the hardware will swap ping-pong buffers and interrupt the software.
Low_Requirements: Setting Data Timeout in DCIDuring nominal operation, frames arrive approximately every 11 msecs.  The DCI hardware will generate an interrupt if event data has been received in one of the Ping-Pong buffers, and a configurable amount of time has passed during which no additional events have been received.  A data timeout interrupt is not an error, but rather typically indicates that the current exposure has been completed.  The data timeout interrupt allows the TMALI CSC to retrieve the trailing events from the DCI Ping-Pong buffer.  The function tmaliDciTimeoutSet() provides an API to the DCI Driver to allow the timeout setting to be altered.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how the data timeout is configured and used in the DCI during nominal operation.
Emphasis & Caution: Clarify that the timeout interrupt is not an error.
Things to Avoid: Do not describe the timeout as a fault condition.

High_Requirements: The DPU-SCUI  shall be capable of packing created CCSDS Source Packets into STPDUs using segmented (grouped) packets for transfer to the SCU as defined by CCSDS-102.0-B-4 and in accordance with interface control document 1143-EI-S19121.
Low_Requirements: Structural DesignThe TIS CSC provides a library of functions to* initialize the DPU clock hardware at startup,* maintain synchronization of the DPU clock with the Spacecraft clock,* provide a function for use by other applications to retrieve the current values of the DPU clock and the UT Delta, and provide functions for use by the CCM command dispatcher to alter the configuration of the clock interface.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, outline the functional responsibilities provided by the TIS CSC clock library.
Emphasis & Caution: Include all clock-related services listed.
Things to Avoid: Do not add unrelated system functions.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall provide the capability for an application program to install a callback routine to respond to the Timeout Interrupt Condition (INTTO_EN).
Low_Requirements: Startup Sequence On flight, a power cycle can only be effected by the SCU.  A watchdog reset could be induced by the DPU FSW due to a detected, unrecoverable error, or it could occur as a by-product of the FSW entering a state in which it fails to strobe the watchdog timer.  On boot, the bootstrap tests and clears DRAM, and then proceeds to load the DPU FSW from EEPROM and executes it.  The DPU FSW then loads configuration information from EEPROM (which establishes various operational defaults) and spawns the various DPU FSW tasks.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe the DPU startup sequence from reset through task creation.
Emphasis & Caution: Distinguish between bootstrap actions and FSW actions.
Things to Avoid: Do not alter the order of the startup steps.

High_Requirements: The DPU compression software shall report errors to the DPU-CCM for inclusion in the DPU Housekeeping information.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine is called by any CSC in order to report an error or event that should be included in DPU housekeeping.  If this routine is called from interrupt context a static global variable, ccmISRError, is set so that the error can be enqueued later (see ccmCtrlTask()).  This is done since the error/event queue is semaphore-protected and a semaphore cannot be taken in an ISR.  The error queue semaphore has priority inversion set to reduce conflicts between multiple callers should a priority inversion situation arise. This routine also replaces frequently occuring errors with a special repeat error code.  The repeat error code is a special error code that follows a normally reported error code to indicate that the normally reported error code previously reported has occurred more than once in the last high rate reporting period
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how public error-reporting functions handle errors from task and interrupt contexts.
Emphasis & Caution: Note ISR limitations and repeat error handling.
Things to Avoid: Do not suggest taking semaphores in an ISR.

High_Requirements: The DPU-TMALI shall utilize SCM_DCI_SR, along with ERRNO provided by DPU-DCI to decode errors and place them on an error queue for DPU-CCM.
Low_Requirements: Error Collection and ReportingThe DPU-CCM CSC provides a centralized error reporting interface, ccmErrEnq(), that other FSW tasks use to report errors.  Each time it wakes, ccmTask() checks to see if it is time to form an error/event packet for transmission to the ground.  If so, ccmTask() calls ccmHkMkError() to actually create the packet and forward it to DPU-SCUI for transmission to the ground.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe the centralized error collection and reporting flow in the DPU-CCM CSC.
Emphasis & Caution: Identify when packets are formed and sent to ground.
Things to Avoid: Do not confuse enqueueing with packet transmission.

High_Requirements: The DPU-BOOT CSC shall wait a minimum of C msecs after a write to EEPROM before performing a read from EEPROM.
Low_Requirements: Hardware InterfacesThe DPU-BOOTSTRAP must interface directly with the EEPROM on the Company X Communication Module.  The DPU-BOOTSTRAP accesses EEPROM as ?-bit words only.  The specific timing requirements of the EEPROM are satisfied in one of three ways:* the timing requirements are met by inserting specific wait loops (typical),* the timing requirements are met by use of the EEPROM Driver (DOWNLOAD command), andthe timing requirements are met implicitly by the amount of time which will be required to execute the intervening instructions regardless of the CPU speed setting (DRAM test logic).
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, restate how the DPU-BOOTSTRAP interfaces with EEPROM and meets EEPROM timing constraints.
Emphasis & Caution: Ensure consistency with word size access and timing methods.
Things to Avoid: Do not introduce new interface assumptions.

High_Requirements: The DPU-SCUI shall time stamp the secondary header of the Source Packets with the current SC_TIME retrieved from the DPU-TIS module.
Low_Requirements: Time SynchronizationThe interface with the spacecraft as described in document 1143-EI-S19121 specifies that the spacecraft will provide a Clock Message to each Instrument at a 1 Hz rate.  The Clock Message contains both two values: a value representing the current spacecraft time, and a value representing the delta from the spacecraft time and the Univeral Time (UT), based on an epoch of TBD.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize how spacecraft clock messages are used for time synchronization.
Emphasis & Caution: Include both spacecraft time and UT delta values.
Things to Avoid: Do not change the update rate or message contents.

High_Requirements: The DPU-EDAC shall address hardware modules as defined in document 1400, Company X Specification for the Company X Communication/Memory Module, and document IBM-FAULT-MGMT, RSC System: Fault Handling and Storage Management.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine provides a means to set the bit arrangement used by the DCI.  When bSpecialArrangement is TRUE, the DCI is set to special bit arrangement mode, otherwise the default mode of no rearrangement is set.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how to configure the DCI bit arrangement using the provided public function.
Emphasis & Caution: Clearly define behavior when special arrangement is enabled or disabled.
Things to Avoid: Do not reverse the meaning of the boolean flag.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall provide an application program with a function to read the contents of the ping/pong buffer.
Low_Requirements: Event ProcessingIf the Mode command is Instrument Y Mode, the detector events are spatially filtered to the Instrument Y window specified in the Mode command then the corresponding pixel in the Ping or Pong buffer is incremented.  If specified in the Mode command, pixels will be binned at AxA or BxB.  Also, if specified in the Mode command, a tracking frame data product will be produced for every Ping/Pong tracking frame. Instrument Y and Event Mode may be commanded simultaneously in the Mode command. If the ICU sends an XRT Position command, the Instrument Y mode and Event Mode windows will be resized to match the new window sizes in the command.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how detector events are processed in Instrument Y and Event Modes.
Emphasis & Caution: Handle window resizing, binning options, and Ping/Pong buffering correctly.
Things to Avoid: Do not ignore Mode command options.

High_Requirements: The DPU-BOOT CSC shall create a record of failed DRAM blocks, BIT_DRAM, in the SYSTEM_BLOCK.
Low_Requirements: Exclude Failed DRAMThe Bootstrap performs a memory test before loading the operating system.  The results of this test are stored in the EEPROM.  The DPU-RTOS uses the results of the memory test to exclude memory segments with bit errors from the system memory pool. The Bootstrap constructs a bit map which effectively implements a list of failed memory blocks during the bootstrap built-in test.  A set of functions have been created to utilize this  bit map to exclude failed memory blocks from the VxWorks system memory pool.  The system memory pool is used by VxWorks to allocate memory dynamically.  In addition, a linked list of good memory segments is created for use by the EDAC Memory Scrubber.  These functions are called from the usrInit() and usrRoot() functions in usrConfig.c.  The dramInit() function must be called prior to kernelInit() in usrInit().  The dramBuildMemPool() function is called just prior to initialization of filesystems and drivers in usrRoot().
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how failed DRAM is identified and excluded from the system memory pool.
Emphasis & Caution: Note function call order and separation of good vs failed memory.
Things to Avoid: Do not change the initialization sequence.

High_Requirements: The DPU-BOOT CSC shall wait a minimum of C msecs after a write to EEPROM before performing a read from EEPROM.
Low_Requirements: Install Exception HandlersThis routine installs a hook routine for the Memory Error Interrupt.  The hook routine will be called from the interrupt context.  This function returns OK always.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how the memory error interrupt handler is installed.
Emphasis & Caution: Remember the hook runs in interrupt context and always returns OK.
Things to Avoid: Do not add error handling logic.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall write to the DCI Memory in B-bit words only.
Low_Requirements: CPU BITThe CPU BIT consists of a series of tests designed to test each of the functional units of the processor.  These include test for the Branch-Unit, the Fixed-Point Unit, the Floating-Point Unit, the Interrupt-Unit, and the Timer Unit.   Each of these tests consists of executing a few selected instructions from the this functional unit. The General Purpose Registers (GPR), the Segment Registers (SR), the Counter Register (CTR), and the Condition Register are tested for stuck-bits as part of the Branch-Unit test.  This is done by writing a 1s pattern (verified by reading it back) and then writing a 0s pattern (verified by reading it back).
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize the CPU BIT coverage and register testing method.
Emphasis & Caution: Include all functional units and the stuck-bit test process.
Things to Avoid: Do not omit any tested unit.

High_Requirements: The DPU-BIT shall report errors that occur during tests as failures in the test results.
Low_Requirements: Built-In TestThere are two stages of Built-In Tests (BIT) for the DPU.  The Stage 1 BIT is included in the Bootstrap program and consists of Central Processing Unit (CPU) BIT, and Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM) BIT.  The Stage 2 BIT is executed after the operating system is booted and is not part of the Bootstrap program.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain the two stages of Built-In Tests and when each runs.
Emphasis & Caution: Distinguish clearly between Stage 1 and Stage 2.
Things to Avoid: Do not merge the two stages.

High_Requirements: The DPU compression software shall report errors to the DPU-CCM for inclusion in the DPU Housekeeping information.
Low_Requirements: External Program InterfacesThis routine copies the BIT Results into bitHk for inclusion in the DPU Housekeeping.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how BIT results are copied for DPU housekeeping use.
Emphasis & Caution: Ensure results are placed in bitHk.
Things to Avoid: Do not modify the BIT data.

High_Requirements: The Bootstrap Monitor of the DPU-BOOT CSC shall provide the capability to read from Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM), the VME bus, and the Input/Output Channel Controller (IOCC) on command.
Low_Requirements: Structural DesignThe TIS CSC provides a library of functions to* initialize the DPU clock hardware at startup,* maintain synchronization of the DPU clock with the Spacecraft clock,* provide a function for use by other applications to retrieve the current values of the DPU clock and the UT Delta, and provide functions for use by the CCM command dispatcher to alter the configuration of the clock interface.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, outline the clock-related functions provided by the TIS CSC.
Emphasis & Caution: Cover initialization, synchronization, retrieval, and configuration.
Things to Avoid: Do not include non-clock functions.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall address hardware modules as defined in document 1400, Company X Specification for the Company X Communication/Memory Module.
Low_Requirements: Memory Upload and Download HandlingData can be upload to several types of locations, including* DRAM* EEPROM* hardware registers, and* EEPROM filesystem.ial D_MEM_DAT_UPLD command specify the target location.  If the destination is the EEPROM filesystem, a block number is provided in lieu of a memory address , which is used by the DPU FSW to formulate a filename of the form eefs1:DPU_blk.##, where ## is the block number.  In this case, once the entirety of the uploaded data is received by the DPU FSW, the uploaded data is then written to that file in the EEPROM filesystem.  If a file already exists with that name, it is overwritten.  The EEPROM filesystem can be reinitialized using the command D_MEM_DISK_INIT.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how memory upload and download targets are handled.
Emphasis & Caution: Describe EEPROM filesystem behavior and file overwriting.
Things to Avoid: Do not confuse memory addresses with block numbers.

High_Requirements: The DPU-TMALI shall utilize SCM_DCI_SR, along with ERRNO provided by DPU-DCI to decode errors and place them on an error queue for DPU-CCM.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine returns OK, or ERROR and sets errno if the passed parameter is NULL.  The following error codes may be returned in errno
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, state the function return behavior when given a NULL parameter.
Emphasis & Caution: Mention errno usage and possible error codes.
Things to Avoid: Do not change return conditions.

High_Requirements: The channel boundary engineering mode shall create an array of look-up table (LUT) channel boundaries, PROD_CHAN_BOUND, from a flat field, and PROD_MN_DATA, using the algorithm described in Appendix C.
Low_Requirements: Built-In TestThere are two stages of Built-In Tests (BIT) for the DPU.  The Stage 1 BIT is included in the Bootstrap program and consists of Central Processing Unit (CPU) BIT, and Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM) BIT.  The Stage 2 BIT is executed after the operating system is booted and is not part of the Bootstrap program.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, restate the two-stage Built-In Test structure for the DPU.
Emphasis & Caution: Keep consistency with Stage 1 and Stage 2 definitions.
Things to Avoid: Do not introduce new test stages.

High_Requirements: The Bootstrap Monitor of the DPU-BOOT CSC shall output an error message to the RS-232 interface if an incorrect number of command arguments are entered.
Low_Requirements: Sending Messages to the ICUSome special processing and checking is required in this function to handle the transmission of the ACK/NAK message.  When a command arrives from the ICU, the icuiCmdReceiveISR() will verify the message checksum and then send an ACK/NAK message to the ICU.  It is possible that an application is in the midst of sending a message to the ICU at the time a command interrupt occurs.  However, since it is not possible for an ISR to take a semaphore, an additional mechanism is required in order to provide mutual exclusion between a task an the ISR.  To accommodate this problem, the icuiEnq() function first determines whether it is being invoked from interrupt context.  If so, it then tests the value of a static variable, icuiEnqBusy.  If the variable is set, the function will save the given ACK/NAK message parameters in static variables and return.  If the variable is not set, the function will continue as normal (avoiding the semTake() and semGive() calls).  In normal execution, the icuiEnqBusy variable is set on entry and negated on exit, after which the static variables containing the ACK/NAK message parameters will be checked to determine whether an ACK/NAK is pending.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how ACK/NAK messages are safely sent to the ICU during normal and interrupt contexts.
Emphasis & Caution: Handle ISR context, icuiEnqBusy flag, and deferred ACK/NAK correctly.
Things to Avoid: Do not take semaphores in an ISR.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall interpret and execute/dispatch real-time DPU_CMDs.
Low_Requirements: Real-Time Clock InterfaceThis routine gets the value of the Real-Time Clock (RTC) Registers and places the results in variables rtcu and rtcl.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how RTC register values are read and stored.
Emphasis & Caution: Ensure both upper and lower values are captured.
Things to Avoid: Do not omit either register.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DPA will obtain the initial tracking frame time from the ICU.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine provides a means for the CCM task to send the Stop Mode command from the ICU to the DPA software. The Stop Mode command shall be used to command the DPU to immediately stop processing events, complete the current data product, purge events from the DPU hardware, and enter Idle Mode.  The data product will include the actual exposure time and the number of frames.  In response to this command, the DPU shall issue a Mode Complete command to the ICU after finishing the before-mentioned tasks.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how the Stop Mode command is sent and handled from ICU to DPU.
Emphasis & Caution: Include event stopping, data completion, and Mode Complete response.
Things to Avoid: Do not skip required shutdown steps.

High_Requirements: The DPU-EDAC shall record the current contents of EDAC_HK to EEPROM and force a DPU watchdog in the event an MBE in DRAM is detected.
Low_Requirements: types of external (VME) interruptsThe DPU has five types of external (VME) interrupts:* MIL-STD-1553 Interface Interrupts,* DCI Interface Interrupts,* SSI Interface Interrupts,* Memory EDAC Interrupt, and* Ethernet Interrupt (ground use only).
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, list the types of external VME interrupts supported by the DPU.
Emphasis & Caution: Include all five interrupt types.
Things to Avoid: Do not add or remove interrupt categories.

High_Requirements: The DPU-RTOS shall install an hook routine which is invoked on system exception which shall attempt to record the interrupt vector to the LAST_BOOT_IVEC location in EEPROM prior to reboot.
Low_Requirements: Raw Event List (RE) Engineering ModeThe purpose of Raw Event List Engineering Mode is to diagnose problems with the detector. Detector events produced in a given detector mode are collected over a commanded integration time and transmitted to the ground as a list of raw events.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain the purpose and behavior of Raw Event List Engineering Mode.

Definition: In this task, summarize the DRAM BIT process across both boot sequences.

Definition: In this task, describe how telemetry output is synchronized with the spacecraft 1553 bus.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall provide an application program with the ability to read and write the hardware ping-pong buffers when in Test Mode.
Low_Requirements: Setting Frame Depth in DCIThe optimum frame depth will be determined with benchmark testing.  The default frame depth is 10 frames.  Given the above data, it will take approximately 24 msec to read 10 maximum-sized frames at the maximum data transfer rate.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how the DCI frame depth is set and its performance impact.
Emphasis & Caution: State the default frame depth and timing estimate.
Things to Avoid: Do not change benchmark assumptions.

Low_Requirements: Event ProcessingIf the Mode command is Instrument Y Mode, the detector events are spatially filtered to the Instrument Y window specified in the Mode command then the corresponding pixel in the Ping or Pong buffer is incremented.  If specified in the Mode command, pixels will be binned at AxA or BxB.  Also, if specified in the Mode command, a tracking frame data product will be produced for every Ping/Pong tracking frame. Instrument Y and Event Mode may be commanded simultaneously in the Mode command. If the ICU sends an XRT Position command, the Instrument Y mode and Event Mode windows will be resized to match the new window sizes in the command.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how events are processed in Instrument Y and Event Modes.
Emphasis & Caution: Handle windowing, binning, tracking frames, and resizing correctly.
Things to Avoid: Do not ignore Mode command combinations.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall address hardware modules as defined in document 1400, Company X Specification for the Company X Communication/Memory Module.
Low_Requirements: Setting Frame Depth in DCIThe optimum frame depth will be determined with benchmark testing.  The default frame depth is 10 frames.  Given the above data, it will take approximately 24 msec to read 10 maximum-sized frames at the maximum data transfer rate.

Emphasis & Caution: Note behavior differences between NORMAL and TEST modes.

High_Requirements: In image mode, the DPU-DPA shall calculate the memory location to increment for each valid detector event received.  The memory location shall be incremented for each valid event.  Each memory location shall be able to count so many events.  An Image mode exposure shall produce a PROD_IMAGE.
Low_Requirements: Raw Event List (RE) Engineering ModeThe purpose of Raw Event List Engineering Mode is to diagnose problems with the detector. Detector events produced in a given detector mode are collected over a commanded integration time and transmitted to the ground as a list of raw events.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain the purpose and operation of Raw Event List Engineering Mode.
Emphasis & Caution: Focus on diagnostic use and raw event transmission.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall strobe the Watchdog timer  by writing to the SCM_WDR at an interval of 1 second.
Low_Requirements: Control and MonitoringEvery time the CCM Control executes, it calls ccmPerProcess() to handle periodic processing responsibilities.  Such responsibilities include analog to digital conversion updates, DPU task monitoring, ICU heartbeat message production, and watchdog strobe. The ccmHealthChk() function, called by ccmPerProcess() verifies the execution of other tasks by monitoring the amount of time that has elapsed since each task last reported.  Other tasks report their execution to the CCM Control Task by calling the function, ccmTaskReport(), providing their task index.  Each task has an expected execution frequency, and if a task does not execute as expected, an error is reported in DPU housekeeping.  If the Command Dispatch Task fails to report for an extended period, the DPU will execute a reboot, since it is impossible to command the DPU if this task is not executing, otherwise it will strobe the watchdog
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize how CCM Control performs periodic control and health monitoring.
Emphasis & Caution: Highlight task reporting, watchdog handling, and reboot conditions.
Things to Avoid: Do not alter failure criteria.

High_Requirements: The DPU-ICUI shall provide an API for other CSCs to transmit messages to the ICU.
Low_Requirements: Telescope Module Access Library and Interface CSCThe TMALI CSC includes the following components:* A queue, TMALI_EVENT_QUEUE, implemented using a ring buffer, which contains the detector events which have been received over the data capture interface from the DCI Driver, but which have not yet been retrieved by the DPA CSC;* An API which facilitates the retrieval of detector events from the queue by the DPA CSC.  The API consists of the tmaliNextEvent() and tmaliWait() functions.* An set of API functions used by the DPA CSC to configure the DCI interface, including tmaliBitArrangementSet(), tmaliDciFrameDepthGet(), tmaliDciFrameDepthSet(), tmaliDciWindowSet(), tmaliDataTimeoutSet(), and tmaliDataTimeoutGet().* An API housekeeping function, tmaliHkGet(), which gathers  CSC housekeeping data at the request of the Command and Control Task.* The task tmaliTask() and an ISR tmaliTransferToQueueISR() work together via a semaphore to synchronize the transfer of data from the DCI to the  queueAn error handling ISR, tmaliDciErrorReportedISR(), is installed as a hook routine to the DCI Driver, and is used to keep track of the number of errors reported by the DCI driver.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, outline the components and APIs provided by the TMALI CSC.
Emphasis & Caution: Cover event queue, APIs, tasks, ISRs, and error handling.
Things to Avoid: Do not omit any listed interface.

High_Requirements: The DPU-RTOS shall include the capability to dynamically load object modules from the EEPROM.
Low_Requirements: Built-In Test CSCThe Built-In Test (BIT) CSC is a Level 2 reuse component from the SSFF and INSTRUMENT Y projects.  The detailed design of the BIT CSC follows. The Built-In Tests CSC, identified DPU-BIT, performs the Stage 2 Built-In Tests (BIT).  The Stage 2 BIT includes a test of the SCM EDAC circuit, a checksum test on SCM PROM, a MIL-STD-1553B internal BIT, and a memory test of the DCI Data Buffers. The results of the BIT are recorded in the SYS_CNFG_AREA in EEPROM and are also maintained in DRAM.  If an error occurs in one of the functions performing the BIT, the test result will be reported as a failure in the test itself.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe the scope and results handling of the Stage 2 BIT CSC.
Emphasis & Caution: Include all tests and result storage locations.
Things to Avoid: Do not include Stage 1 BIT details.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall provide an application program with the ability to read and write the hardware ping-pong buffers when in Test Mode.
Low_Requirements: Transmit Message Mode(FIOSSIBGTXMSGENABLE) and Inter-Block Gap interruptsThe SSI Driver CSC supports a message mode to facilitate application level communication.  This mode uses the Inter-Block Gap interrupt generated by the hardware to synchronize message transmission. When in a messaging mode, the driver enables an internal Byte Count queue provides a synchronization mechanism for sending messages.  This Byte Count queue is updated with the message size as Inter-Block Gap interrupts are received. The Transmit Message Mode uses the BGTX interrupt, the Tx FIFO Empty Interrupt, the write() call, and an internal Byte Count queue to synchronize the transmission of messages over SSI.  In this mode, the maxBytes parameter passed to write() is enqueued to the Byte Count queue.  When a period of time equal to or greater than the Interblock Gap time has expired, the BGTX interrupt will be asserted.  At this time, the driver will respond and initiate a new transfer by setting a Byte Transfer Limit for this block and enabling the Tx FIFO Empty Interrupt.  The Byte Transfer Limit corresponds to the entry on the Byte Count queue.  When all bytes in the current block have been sent up to the Byte Transfer Limit, the Tx FIFO ISR will disable itself and reset the Byte Transfer Limit to zero. An application may use this mode by calling write() with a buffer pointer and maxBytes in order to transfer a message of a particular length.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how SSI Transmit Message Mode uses interrupts to send messages.
Emphasis & Caution: Describe Byte Count queue, BGTX, and Tx FIFO behavior.
Things to Avoid: Do not change the interrupt-driven sequence.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall provide the capability to echo to the spacecraft each received command from the spacecraft or the ICU.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine provides a means for the CCM task to send the XRT Position command from the ICU to the DPA software. The XRT Position command shall be used to command the DPU to update the position and window parameters that it uses to spatially filter event data.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how the XRT Position command updates event filtering parameters.
Emphasis & Caution: Ensure position and window updates are included.
Things to Avoid: Do not describe unrelated commands.

High_Requirements: The DPU-BOOT CSC shall toggle the BC_INDEX (selecting the opposite configuration) after reading it during the boot process.
Low_Requirements: Transmit Message Mode(FIOSSIBGTXMSGENABLE) and Inter-Block Gap interruptsThe SSI Driver CSC supports a message mode to facilitate application level communication.  This mode uses the Inter-Block Gap interrupt generated by the hardware to synchronize message transmission. When in a messaging mode, the driver enables an internal Byte Count queue provides a synchronization mechanism for sending messages.  This Byte Count queue is updated with the message size as Inter-Block Gap interrupts are received. The Transmit Message Mode uses the BGTX interrupt, the Tx FIFO Empty Interrupt, the write() call, and an internal Byte Count queue to synchronize the transmission of messages over SSI.  In this mode, the maxBytes parameter passed to write() is enqueued to the Byte Count queue.  When a period of time equal to or greater than the Interblock Gap time has expired, the BGTX interrupt will be asserted.  At this time, the driver will respond and initiate a new transfer by setting a Byte Transfer Limit for this block and enabling the Tx FIFO Empty Interrupt.  The Byte Transfer Limit corresponds to the entry on the Byte Count queue.  When all bytes in the current block have been sent up to the Byte Transfer Limit, the Tx FIFO ISR will disable itself and reset the Byte Transfer Limit to zero. An application may use this mode by calling write() with a buffer pointer and maxBytes in order to transfer a message of a particular length.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, restate the SSI Transmit Message Mode synchronization mechanism.
Emphasis & Caution: Keep consistent with Inter-Block Gap and Byte Transfer Limit logic.
Things to Avoid: Do not duplicate or modify queue behavior.

High_Requirements: The DPU-TMALI shall allocate a ring buffer of size specified by TMALI_BUFFER_SIZE provided during initialization for storing incoming frames of data.  The default buffer size shall be 15 MB.
Low_Requirements: DRAM BITPart of the memory is tested in Boot Sequence 1, and part of the memory is tested during Boot Sequence 2.  During Boot Sequence 1, ? KB of DRAM is tested before the Bootstrap is copied to this area.  During Boot Sequence 2, the  remaining memory is tested in ? KB blocks. The DRAM BIT first writes the address of each word in a block to that word in the block.   Then the test flushes the cache and reads back the entire block.  If a single-bit error occurs on the read-back, the location is read again.  If another single-bit error occurs, the test assumes a hard error and marks the whole block bad.  If a multiple-bit error occurs, the whole block is marked bad.  If the value read back does not match the address, the whole block is marked bad.  This same process is repeated with the ones complement of the address. The Bootstrap stores the results of the DRAM BIT in EEPROM as a series of x consecutive y-bit words.  Each bit in a result word represents the result of testing a ? KB block of DRAM.  These bits are mapped to DRAM by proceeding from least-significant bit (LSb) to most-significant bit (MSb) as the addresses of the memory blocks increase.  A set bit indicates that a block of DRAM has failed.  A clear bit indicates that a block of DRAM has passed the test.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize the DRAM BIT process and result encoding.
Emphasis & Caution: Distinguish boot sequences, error types, and bit mapping.
Things to Avoid: Do not confuse pass and fail indicators.

High_Requirements: The DPU-BOOT CSC shall provide a monitor which accepts commands over the RS-232 interface.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsSets the poll rate and buffer rate to the given values.  If the values are out of range, the prior values are retained and an error is reported.  The scuiTask() semaphore timeout (scuiTaskTimeout) is automatically adjusted based on the given poll rate, by setting the timeout to approximately  the poll rate (not that the timeout is expressed in clock ticks, and so the resolution is low and only approximates  of the poll rate).
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how poll rate and buffer rate are set and validated.
Emphasis & Caution: Adjust scuiTaskTimeout based on poll rate and retain prior values if out of range.
Things to Avoid: Do not apply invalid rates.

High_Requirements: The DPU-TIS shall provide the capability for an application program to set the value of the SC_TIME register(s) on the SCM and enable the 1PPS clock jam register, if automatic time synchronization is enabled.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsInitializes the TIS CSC by initializing the hardware register convenience pointers and selecting the commanded 1PPS signal, and enables or disables automatic time synchronization with the S/C according to the passed parameter. THIS FUNCTION ALTERS THE VALUE OF THE DCI CSR AND SHOULD NOT BE INVOKED WHEN DCI INTERRUPTS ARE ACTIVE.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how the TIS CSC is initialized and time sync is configured.
Emphasis & Caution: Do not invoke while DCI interrupts are active.
Things to Avoid: Do not modify DCI CSR during active interrupts.

High_Requirements: The DPU-RTOS shall provide a function to allow an application program to read from the Real-Time Clock registers on the RAD6000SC CPU Module.
Low_Requirements: Startup Sequence On flight, a power cycle can only be effected by the SCU.  A watchdog reset could be induced by the DPU FSW due to a detected, unrecoverable error, or it could occur as a by-product of the FSW entering a state in which it fails to strobe the watchdog timer.  On boot, the bootstrap tests and clears DRAM, and then proceeds to load the DPU FSW from EEPROM and executes it.  The DPU FSW then loads configuration information from EEPROM (which establishes various operational defaults) and spawns the various DPU FSW tasks.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe the full startup sequence from reset to task creation.
Emphasis & Caution: Separate bootstrap actions from FSW actions.
Things to Avoid: Do not change the execution order.

High_Requirements: The Intensifier Characteristics Engineering mode shall produce a pulse-height histogram, PROD_INTENSE_CHAR.
Low_Requirements: Data Processing Algorithm CSCThe DPA CSC includes the following components:* Instrument Y Processor Task  creates histogrammed Instrument Ys, performs Tracking (choose guide stars, drift correction, shift-and-add), creates the Reference, Accumulation, and Data Product buffers, and provides a task heartbeat to the CCM task.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize the responsibilities of the Instrument Y Processor Task in the DPA CSC.

Low_Requirements: Event ProcessingThe Event Processor Task, after receiving a Mode command from the ICU, configures the DPU DCI hardware, responds to the ICU with a Mode Ready message then receives detector events by calling functions tmaliWait() and tmaliNextEvent().  The Event Processor Task will collect detector events for the time period specified in the Mode command then issue a Mode Complete message to the ICU.  After issuing the Mode Complete message, the Event Processor Task will enter the Idle state.

Emphasis & Caution: Include Mode Ready, event collection, and Mode Complete.

High_Requirements: There shall be four engineering modes associated with the detector: raw event list mode, channel boundary mode, centroid confirmation mode, and intensifier characteristics mode.
Low_Requirements: Raw Event List (RE) Engineering ModeThe purpose of Raw Event List Engineering Mode is to diagnose problems with the detector. Detector events produced in a given detector mode are collected over a commanded integration time and transmitted to the ground as a list of raw events.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain the purpose and behavior of Raw Event List Engineering Mode.

High_Requirements: The DPU-SCUI  shall construct CCSDS Source Packets from Data Packages as defined by CCSDS-102.0-B-4, and in accordance with interface control document 1143-EI-S19121.
Low_Requirements: Raw Event List (RE) Engineering ModeThe purpose of Raw Event List Engineering Mode is to diagnose problems with the detector. Detector events produced in a given detector mode are collected over a commanded integration time and transmitted to the ground as a list of raw events.

High_Requirements: The Bootstrap Monitor of the DPU-BOOT CSC shall validate commands entered for syntax and number of arguments.
Low_Requirements: Bootstrap MonitorThe Bootstrap Monitor checks entered commands for syntax and number of arguments, and displays an error message to the RS-232 interface if an invalid command or argument is entered.  A complete listing of these messages is given in document 7384-BSPS-01.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how the Bootstrap Monitor validates commands and reports errors.
Emphasis & Caution: Use RS-232 for error messages and follow defined syntax rules.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall provide a mechanism for other CSCs to report errors for inclusion in the DPU_HK.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine is called by any CSC in order to report an error or event that should be included in DPU housekeeping.  If this routine is called from interrupt context a static global variable, ccmISRError, is set so that the error can be enqueued later (see ccmCtrlTask()).  This is done since the error/event queue is semaphore-protected and a semaphore cannot be taken in an ISR.  The error queue semaphore has priority inversion set to reduce conflicts between multiple callers should a priority inversion situation arise. This routine also replaces frequently occuring errors with a special repeat error code.  The repeat error code is a special error code that follows a normally reported error code to indicate that the normally reported error code previously reported has occurred more than once in the last high rate reporting period
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how errors/events are reported to DPU housekeeping from tasks and ISRs.
Emphasis & Caution: Handle ISR context without taking semaphores and use repeat error codes.
Things to Avoid: Do not enqueue directly from an ISR.

High_Requirements: The DPU-RTOS shall install an hook routine which is invoked on system exception which shall attempt to record the interrupt vector to the LAST_BOOT_IVEC location in EEPROM prior to reboot.
Low_Requirements: Install Exception HandlersThis routine installs a hook routine for the Power Failure Interrupt.  The hook routine will be called from the interrupt context.  This function returns OK always.
Instruction:

Low_Requirements: Telescope Module Access Library and Interface CSCThe Telescope Module Access Library and Interface (TMALI) CSC manages the detector event queue and provides an API for the retrieval of detector events from that queue to the data processing algorithms.
Instruction:

Low_Requirements: Instrument Y ProcessingAt the end of an exposure, the Instrument Y in the accumulation buffer is transferred to the Instrument Y data product buffer one horizontal line at a time, starting at the top of the Instrument Y to form a PROD_INSTRUMENT Y data product.  This rasterized Instrument Y in the Instrument Y data product buffer will be enqueued for transmission to the spacecraft.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe the step-by-step process of transferring Instrument Y data from accumulation buffer to data product buffer and enqueueing it for transmission.
Emphasis & Caution: Preserve the top-to-bottom, line-by-line raster order.

Low_Requirements: DRAM BITPart of the memory is tested in Boot Sequence 1, and part of the memory is tested during Boot Sequence 2.  During Boot Sequence 1, ? KB of DRAM is tested before the Bootstrap is copied to this area.  During Boot Sequence 2, the  remaining memory is tested in ? KB blocks. The DRAM BIT first writes the address of each word in a block to that word in the block.   Then the test flushes the cache and reads back the entire block.  If a single-bit error occurs on the read-back, the location is read again.  If another single-bit error occurs, the test assumes a hard error and marks the whole block bad.  If a multiple-bit error occurs, the whole block is marked bad.  If the value read back does not match the address, the whole block is marked bad.  This same process is repeated with the ones complement of the address. The Bootstrap stores the results of the DRAM BIT in EEPROM as a series of x consecutive y-bit words.  Each bit in a result word represents the result of testing a ? KB block of DRAM.  These bits are mapped to DRAM by proceeding from least-significant bit (LSb) to most-significant bit (MSb) as the addresses of the memory blocks increase.  A set bit indicates that a block of DRAM has failed.  A clear bit indicates that a block of DRAM has passed the test.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, concisely explain the DRAM BIT process across Boot Sequence 1 and 2, including test logic, error handling, and result storage.
Emphasis & Caution: Clearly distinguish single-bit vs multiple-bit errors and block marking rules.
Things to Avoid: Omitting result bit mapping or EEPROM storage.

High_Requirements: If commanded, during the collection of event data, the DPU-DPA shall produce PROD_ACS_MSG.
Low_Requirements: SCU Interface CSCThis CSC performs rate-buffering of the downlink telemetry to the SCU according to a commandable parameter.  The rate at which the SCU polls the DPU is also commandable.  The CSC installs an ISR to collect telecommands from the SCU via the MILSTD1553B Interface.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, outline the SCU Interface CSC responsibilities for telemetry rate-buffering, polling control, and telecommand collection via MIL-STD-1553B.
Emphasis & Caution: Note that rates and polling are commandable.
Things to Avoid: Describing unrelated interfaces.

High_Requirements: The DPU-TIS shall provide the capability for application programs to access the current UT_DELTA.
Low_Requirements: Control and Monitoringthe CCM Control Task also handles memory dump commands.  In the event of a data dump command, the CCM Control Task will break the dump into manageable pieces and dump a small portion at a time, each time the task is awakened.  The purpose of this deferred activity is to prevent a large dump from consuming available CPU time by keeping the high-priority CCM Command Dispatch Task busy for an extended period.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how the CCM Control Task handles memory dump commands using deferred, segmented dumping.
Emphasis & Caution: Emphasize CPU time protection and task priority rationale.
Things to Avoid: Suggesting large dumps occur in a single operation.

High_Requirements: The DPU-TIS shall provide the capability for application programs to access the SC_TIME from the SCM.
Low_Requirements: Exclude Failed DRAMThe Bootstrap performs a memory test before loading the operating system.  The results of this test are stored in the EEPROM.  The DPU-RTOS uses the results of the memory test to exclude memory segments with bit errors from the system memory pool. The Bootstrap constructs a bit map which effectively implements a list of failed memory blocks during the bootstrap built-in test.  A set of functions have been created to utilize this  bit map to exclude failed memory blocks from the VxWorks system memory pool.  The system memory pool is used by VxWorks to allocate memory dynamically.  In addition, a linked list of good memory segments is created for use by the EDAC Memory Scrubber.  These functions are called from the usrInit() and usrRoot() functions in usrConfig.c.  The dramInit() function must be called prior to kernelInit() in usrInit().  The dramBuildMemPool() function is called just prior to initialization of filesystems and drivers in usrRoot().
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize how failed DRAM blocks are identified, excluded from the VxWorks memory pool, and prepared for EDAC scrubbing during bootstrap.
Emphasis & Caution: Respect the required call order in usrInit() and usrRoot().
Things to Avoid: Calling dramInit() after kernelInit().

High_Requirements: The DPU-SSI shall provide the capability for an application program to enable/disable the VME Slave Interrupt on the SCM for the SSI Interface.
Low_Requirements: Setting Data Timeout in DCIDuring nominal operation, frames arrive approximately every 11 msecs.  The DCI hardware will generate an interrupt if event data has been received in one of the Ping-Pong buffers, and a configurable amount of time has passed during which no additional events have been received.  A data timeout interrupt is not an error, but rather typically indicates that the current exposure has been completed.  The data timeout interrupt allows the TMALI CSC to retrieve the trailing events from the DCI Ping-Pong buffer.  The function tmaliDciTimeoutSet() provides an API to the DCI Driver to allow the timeout setting to be altered.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how data timeout is used in the DCI to signal exposure completion and retrieve trailing events, including the configuration API.
Emphasis & Caution: State that data timeout is not an error condition.
Things to Avoid: Treating the timeout interrupt as a fault.

High_Requirements: The DPU-BOOT CSC shall set the processor speed  to MHz.
Low_Requirements: Built-In TestThere are two stages of Built-In Tests (BIT) for the DPU.  The Stage 1 BIT is included in the Bootstrap program and consists of Central Processing Unit (CPU) BIT, and Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM) BIT.  The Stage 2 BIT is executed after the operating system is booted and is not part of the Bootstrap program.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, distinguish Stage 1 and Stage 2 Built-In Tests and when each is executed.
Emphasis & Caution: Identify which tests are included in the Bootstrap.
Things to Avoid: Mixing Bootstrap and post-boot responsibilities.

High_Requirements: The DPU-BOOT CSC shall complete Stage 1 BIT, and load DPU-RTOS in less than A seconds following a hardware reset if CTS is not asserted.
Low_Requirements: initial boot sequencethe initial boot sequence, identified as Boot Sequence 1.  This processing takes place when the Bootstrap executes from Programmable Read-Only Memory (PROM).  Boot Sequence 1 is composed primarily of 4 steps as follows.First, in this sequence, EEPROM is updated.  Then, CPU BIT and a portion of DRAM BIT is performed.  Next, DRAM is cleared.  Lastly, Boot Sequence 2 is copied to DRAM and executed. During this sequence, the rest of DRAM is tested if skipBit is not set.  Load parameters are put into registers r13, r14, r15 and r16.  DRAM is cleared up to memSize.  Lastly, either the DPU-RTOS or the Bootstrap Monitor is loaded and executed according to the load parameters.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, outline the steps of the initial boot sequence (Boot Sequence 1) from PROM execution through loading the OS or monitor.
Emphasis & Caution: Maintain the correct order of EEPROM update, BITs, DRAM clearing, and loading.
Things to Avoid: Reordering boot steps or omitting load parameters.

High_Requirements: The DPU-BOOT CSC shall enable power to the EEPROM on the SCM.
Low_Requirements: Standard I/O Interface The write primitive does not block.  The write() primitive is managed by the tyLib function, tyWrite() when the driver is not in BGTX Message Mode.  The size of the Tx ring buffer is specified in the call to ssiDevCreate() as part of the device instantiation. This routine returns either the number of bytes written, or ERROR.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain the behavior of the standard I/O write primitive, including non-blocking behavior, buffer management, and return values.
Emphasis & Caution: Note that write size depends on the Tx ring buffer.
Things to Avoid: Claiming the write() call blocks.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DCI shall read from the DCI Registers in C-bit words only
Low_Requirements: Standard I/O InterfaceThe read() primitive does not block.  A read is performed on the active portion of the H/W ping pong buffer using the RAD 6000 move-multiple instruction, referred to here after as a block move.  For optimum performance, the block move is implemented in assembler.  The number of bytes read is returned to the caller.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize the non-blocking read() behavior, block move mechanism, and returned data size.
Emphasis & Caution: Note that read() does not block and uses a block move.
Things to Avoid: Claiming blocking behavior or ignoring return value.

Low_Requirements: Full-Frame (FF) Engineering ModeThe purpose of Full-Frame Engineering Mode is to monitor the health of the detector to locate hot spots and dead pixels. Detector events produced in Full Frame High Resolution detector format are received and histogrammed for a commanded integration time.  The entire Instrument Y is transmitted to the ground.  This mode can use high resolution (all pixels) or low resolution imaging (BxB , DxD pixel binning).
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe the goal and data flow of Full-Frame Engineering Mode for detector health monitoring.
Emphasis & Caution: Include integration time, histogramming, and resolution options.
Things to Avoid: Omitting full Instrument Y transmission.

High_Requirements: The DPU-ICUI shall use DPU-SSI to communicate with the SSI Interface.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine initializes the interface to the ICU.  This routine opens SSI Driver port, installs the SSI interface interrupt callbacks, and enables Transmit Message Mode in the SSI Driver.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how the routine initializes the ICU interface and configures the SSI Driver.
Emphasis & Caution: Mention port opening, callbacks, and Transmit Message Mode.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall maintain counts of both the number of commands successfully received and rejected and report the parameters in DPU_HK.
Low_Requirements: CPU BITThe CPU BIT consists of a series of tests designed to test each of the functional units of the processor.  These include test for the Branch-Unit, the Fixed-Point Unit, the Floating-Point Unit, the Interrupt-Unit, and the Timer Unit.   Each of these tests consists of executing a few selected instructions from the this functional unit. The General Purpose Registers (GPR), the Segment Registers (SR), the Counter Register (CTR), and the Condition Register are tested for stuck-bits as part of the Branch-Unit test.  This is done by writing a 1s pattern (verified by reading it back) and then writing a 0s pattern (verified by reading it back).
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, concisely explain the purpose and scope of the CPU BIT and its tested units and registers.
Emphasis & Caution: Distinguish functional unit tests and stuck-bit checks.
Things to Avoid: Skipping tested units.

Instruction:

Emphasis & Caution: Include both frame limit and timeout triggers.

High_Requirements: The DPU-SCUI shall be capable of receiving a telecommand from the SCU every N ms and forward it to DPU-CCM for processing.
Low_Requirements: Command and Control CSCThe Command and Control (CCM) CSC is a Level 2 reuse component from the INSTRUMENT Y project.  The Command and Control CSC includes the following components:* A Control Task, ccmCtrlTask(), which initializes the DPU FSW and spawns other tasks at bootup, schedules the production of DPU housekeeping data packets, monitors the execution of other tasks, and schedules the execution of other periodic tasks such as the heartbeat message and the watchdog strobe; and* A Command Dispatch Task, ccmCmdTask(), which receives and dispatches real-time commands received from the SCU or the ICU.Major data structures include:* A Static Data table that keeps track of the operational state.  Data included in this table includes housekeeping production rates, CCM specific flags, and the number of commands executed.* A Command Queue into which commands are placed when they arrive, via interrupt, from the ICU or the SCU.* An Error/Event Queue which accumulates error and event codes which are reported by the DPU FSW.  These error and event codes are removed from the queue and placed into a telemetry packet at a given interval and included in DPU housekeeping data.DPUSDS5.12.1.1.1Flight Software InitializationThe Command and Control CSC is initialized by spawning the CCM Control Task, ccmCtrlTask(), from the operating system startup task, usrRoot().  After the task is spawned it calls a function, ccmInit(), which creates the error/event queue, instantiates needed semaphores and installs various ISRs.  Finally, it spawns the remaining tasks which comprise the DPU FSW. When the CCM Control Task starts up, it reads DPU configuration startup defaults from the SYSTEM_CONFIG_AREA in EEPROM.  If the SYSTEM_CONFIG_AREA checksum is bad, hard-coded defaults are used.  The CCM Control Task initializes the remaining CSCs by calling the applicable initialization function, or if the CSC has an associated task, by spawning the task using the VxWorks function, taskSpawn().  When the CCM Control Task initializes a CSC, it passes the startup defaults read from EEPROM as parameters to the task initialization function. In addition to its task initialization activities, ccmInit(), also initializes the command length verification table by calling ccmCmdLengthInit().  The command processor uses the created table to verify expected command lengths for newly received commands.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize the structure, tasks, data structures, and initialization flow of the Command and Control CSC.
Emphasis & Caution: Preserve task roles and initialization order.
Things to Avoid: Mixing control and dispatch task responsibilities.

High_Requirements: The DPU-ICUI shall install an ISR to be invoked on the SSI receiver interblock gap interrupt, for the receiving and processing of commands from the ICU.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine is called by the MIL-STD-1553 Command ISR and the SSI Interface ISR any time a command arrives from the SCU or the ICU, to enqueue the command and to wakeup the CCM Command Dispatch Task so that the command can be executed.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall quietly discard any unrecognized observatory messages (I.e. messages which do not have a DPU command APID).
Low_Requirements: Instrument Control Unit Interface CSCThe Instrument Control Unit Interface (ICUI) CSC is a new component that provides support for application-level communication with the INSTRUMENT X ICU.  The ICUI CSC is primarily responsible for implementing the application protocol with the ICU as defined in document DPUICD-01. In order to meet these objectives, ICUI provides: * An ICU Interface Message Send Function, icuiEnq(), which provides an API by which other CSCs can send messages to the ICU.* An ICU Interface Command Reader ISR, icuiCmdReceiveISR(), which receives Telecommands from the ICU and forwards them to the Command and Control Task for processing.* An ICU Interface Housekeeping Function, icuiHkGet(), which gathers ICU Interface housekeeping data at the request of the Command and Control Task.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, outline the responsibilities and provided APIs of the ICUI CSC for communication with the ICU.
Emphasis & Caution: Include send, receive ISR, and housekeeping functions.
Things to Avoid: Referencing protocols outside DPUICD-01.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall record an error to the LAST_BOOT_IVEC location in EEPROM and discontinue strobing the Watchdog Timer should an unrecoverable software error occur.  An unrecoverable software error is defined as an error that causes a loss of commandability or ground communication.
Low_Requirements: Control and MonitoringEvery time the CCM Control executes, it calls ccmPerProcess() to handle periodic processing responsibilities.  Such responsibilities include analog to digital conversion updates, DPU task monitoring, ICU heartbeat message production, and watchdog strobe. The ccmHealthChk() function, called by ccmPerProcess() verifies the execution of other tasks by monitoring the amount of time that has elapsed since each task last reported.  Other tasks report their execution to the CCM Control Task by calling the function, ccmTaskReport(), providing their task index.  Each task has an expected execution frequency, and if a task does not execute as expected, an error is reported in DPU housekeeping.  If the Command Dispatch Task fails to report for an extended period, the DPU will execute a reboot, since it is impossible to command the DPU if this task is not executing, otherwise it will strobe the watchdog
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize how the CCM Control performs periodic processing and monitors task health.
Emphasis & Caution: Note reboot condition if Command Dispatch Task fails.
Things to Avoid: Ignoring task reporting or watchdog behavior.

High_Requirements: The DPU-ICUI shall report errors to DPU-CCM for inclusion in the DPU_HK.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine provides a means for the CCM task to send a Mode command from the ICU to the DPA software. The Mode command shall be used to command the DPU into a particular science or engineering event processing mode.  The mode shall become effective after the DPU has sent the Mode Ready message to the ICU.
Instruction:

Emphasis & Caution: Mode is effective only after Mode Ready is sent.

High_Requirements: The DPU-SSI shall provide the capability for an application program to independently mask/unmask each of the SSI Interrupt Conditions using the SCM_SSI_CSR.
Low_Requirements: Data ProcessingData requiring compression is received via the dcxEnq() function, which has an API definition which matches the scuiEnq() API.  The DCX CSC handles the PKG_CTRL flags as described below:* PKG_COMPRESS  this flag is set to indicate if the package is actually compressed.* PKG_PRIORITY_HIGH  this flag is ignored by the DCX CSC but is forwarded to the SCUI CSC.* PKG_SEGMENT  this flag is ignored by the DCX CSC but is forwarded to the SCUI CSC.PKG_CHECKSUM  if set, the dcxEnq() function will compute a checksum which is appended to the data, and then the flag is negated.  The checksum will be compressed with the rest of the data, so that when the data is decompressed on the ground, the checksum can be recomputed and compared to provide a good indication of whether the decompressed copy of the data matches the original data set.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how DCX processes data for compression and handles PKG_CTRL flags.
Emphasis & Caution: Describe checksum computation and flag negation.
Things to Avoid: Misstating ignored vs forwarded flags.

High_Requirements: The DPU-ICUI shall install an ISR to be invoked on the SSI receiver interblock gap interrupt, for the receiving and processing of commands from the ICU.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine initializes the interface to the ICU.  This routine opens SSI Driver port, installs the SSI interface interrupt callbacks, and enables Transmit Message Mode in the SSI Driver.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how the routine initializes the ICU interface via the SSI Driver.
Emphasis & Caution: Include port open, callbacks, and transmit mode.
Things to Avoid: Adding unrelated initialization steps.

High_Requirements: The DPU-SCUI shall time stamp the secondary header of the Source Packets with the current SC_TIME retrieved from the DPU-TIS module.
Low_Requirements: Time SynchronizationThe time contained in the Clock Message is specified to be valid on the rising edge of the next 1PPS signal.  When the DPU receives the Clock Message, the 1553 ISR calls the tisTimeSync() function, forwarding the data portion of the clock message to the TIS CSC via the TIS_CLK_MSG data structure..  The tisTimeSync() function will immediately copy the spacecraft clock value to the DPU clock hardware registers. The DPU hardware is designed to latch in the new time on the rising edge of the next 1 PPS signal (the act of writing a new time to the hardware registers arms the latch function in the hardware).  Once the new time is latched, the hardware automatically disables the time latch function to avoid the potential of latching in an old time at the next 1 PPS signal.will be received within one second.  In the unlikely event a UT Delta is discarded, an event message is enqueued to ccmErrEnq() for informational purposes.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, describe how spacecraft time is synchronized using the Clock Message and 1PPS.
Emphasis & Caution: Time latches on the next rising 1PPS edge.
Things to Avoid: Suggesting repeated or delayed latching.

High_Requirements: The DPU-ICUI shall provide an API for other CSCs to transmit messages to the ICU.
Low_Requirements: DRAM BITPart of the memory is tested in Boot Sequence 1, and part of the memory is tested during Boot Sequence 2.  During Boot Sequence 1, ? KB of DRAM is tested before the Bootstrap is copied to this area.  During Boot Sequence 2, the  remaining memory is tested in ? KB blocks. The DRAM BIT first writes the address of each word in a block to that word in the block.   Then the test flushes the cache and reads back the entire block.  If a single-bit error occurs on the read-back, the location is read again.  If another single-bit error occurs, the test assumes a hard error and marks the whole block bad.  If a multiple-bit error occurs, the whole block is marked bad.  If the value read back does not match the address, the whole block is marked bad.  This same process is repeated with the ones complement of the address. The Bootstrap stores the results of the DRAM BIT in EEPROM as a series of x consecutive y-bit words.  Each bit in a result word represents the result of testing a ? KB block of DRAM.  These bits are mapped to DRAM by proceeding from least-significant bit (LSb) to most-significant bit (MSb) as the addresses of the memory blocks increase.  A set bit indicates that a block of DRAM has failed.  A clear bit indicates that a block of DRAM has passed the test.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, concisely explain the DRAM BIT process, error handling, and result mapping.
Emphasis & Caution: Distinguish single-bit vs multiple-bit error outcomes.
Things to Avoid: Omitting EEPROM result storage.

High_Requirements: The DPU-DPA shall be capable of providing DPA_HK on request.
Low_Requirements: Telescope Module Access Library and Interface CSCThe TMALI CSC includes the following components:* A queue, TMALI_EVENT_QUEUE, implemented using a ring buffer, which contains the detector events which have been received over the data capture interface from the DCI Driver, but which have not yet been retrieved by the DPA CSC;* An API which facilitates the retrieval of detector events from the queue by the DPA CSC.  The API consists of the tmaliNextEvent() and tmaliWait() functions.* An set of API functions used by the DPA CSC to configure the DCI interface, including tmaliBitArrangementSet(), tmaliDciFrameDepthGet(), tmaliDciFrameDepthSet(), tmaliDciWindowSet(), tmaliDataTimeoutSet(), and tmaliDataTimeoutGet().* An API housekeeping function, tmaliHkGet(), which gathers  CSC housekeeping data at the request of the Command and Control Task.* The task tmaliTask() and an ISR tmaliTransferToQueueISR() work together via a semaphore to synchronize the transfer of data from the DCI to the  queueAn error handling ISR, tmaliDciErrorReportedISR(), is installed as a hook routine to the DCI Driver, and is used to keep track of the number of errors reported by the DCI driver.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize the components, APIs, and data flow of the TMALI CSC.
Emphasis & Caution: Include queue, ISR-task synchronization, and error ISR.
Things to Avoid: Excluding configuration or housekeeping APIs.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall notify the ICU upon completion of boot and flight software initialization.
Low_Requirements: Startup Sequence On flight, a power cycle can only be effected by the SCU.  A watchdog reset could be induced by the DPU FSW due to a detected, unrecoverable error, or it could occur as a by-product of the FSW entering a state in which it fails to strobe the watchdog timer.  On boot, the bootstrap tests and clears DRAM, and then proceeds to load the DPU FSW from EEPROM and executes it.  The DPU FSW then loads configuration information from EEPROM (which establishes various operational defaults) and spawns the various DPU FSW tasks.

Definition: In this task, outline the startup sequence from reset through FSW task spawning.
Emphasis & Caution: Note watchdog reset and EEPROM loading.

High_Requirements: The DPU-CCM shall be able to count a consecutively reported error.  When the the count for a particular error ID, exceeds 250 for a particular reporting period, the error code will be replaced with a error code sequence which shall include the original error code and the number of times the error was reported.
Low_Requirements: Public FunctionsThis routine is called by any CSC in order to report an error or event that should be included in DPU housekeeping.  If this routine is called from interrupt context a static global variable, ccmISRError, is set so that the error can be enqueued later (see ccmCtrlTask()).  This is done since the error/event queue is semaphore-protected and a semaphore cannot be taken in an ISR.  The error queue semaphore has priority inversion set to reduce conflicts between multiple callers should a priority inversion situation arise. This routine also replaces frequently occuring errors with a special repeat error code.  The repeat error code is a special error code that follows a normally reported error code to indicate that the normally reported error code previously reported has occurred more than once in the last high rate reporting period
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, explain how errors and events are reported and queued for housekeeping.
Emphasis & Caution: Note ISR handling via deferred enqueue and repeat error code.
Things to Avoid: Taking semaphores inside an ISR.

High_Requirements: The DPU-BOOT CSC shall create a record of failed DRAM blocks, BIT_DRAM, in the SYSTEM_BLOCK.
Low_Requirements: Built-In Test CSCThe Built-In Test (BIT) CSC is a Level 2 reuse component from the SSFF and INSTRUMENT Y projects.  The detailed design of the BIT CSC follows. The Built-In Tests CSC, identified DPU-BIT, performs the Stage 2 Built-In Tests (BIT).  The Stage 2 BIT includes a test of the SCM EDAC circuit, a checksum test on SCM PROM, a MIL-STD-1553B internal BIT, and a memory test of the DCI Data Buffers. The results of the BIT are recorded in the SYS_CNFG_AREA in EEPROM and are also maintained in DRAM.  If an error occurs in one of the functions performing the BIT, the test result will be reported as a failure in the test itself.
Instruction:
Definition: In this task, summarize the role, tests, and result handling of the BIT CSC.
Emphasis & Caution: Identify Stage 2 BIT scope and result storage locations.
